title: VPN Before You Travel: The Pre-Trip Setup Guide for Restrictive Countries
description: Once you land in China, UAE, or Russia, you may not be able to download a VPN or sign up for one. This is the complete setup checklist to run before you board the plane.
content:
## The One Rule That Overrides Everything Else

Install and test your VPN before you leave. Not at the airport. Not on the layover. Before you get in the taxi to the airport.

In China, the App Store removes VPN apps from its Chinese regional catalog. You can't download NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Proton VPN from an App Store with a Chinese region account. In Russia, VPN provider websites like nordvpn.com and protonvpn.com are blocked at the ISP level. In the UAE, some VPN provider sites are inaccessible on Etisalat and Du. In all three countries, if you arrive without a VPN installed, your options collapse to "find a foreign tourist who has one" or "do without."

Everything in this guide builds on that foundation.

## Step 1: Know What You're Actually Heading Into

Different countries block different things. Your VPN needs depend on what you need:

**China**: Nearly everything is blocked. Google, Gmail, Google Maps, YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, WhatsApp, Twitter/X, Slack, Dropbox, many news sites. You need a VPN for basic communication and navigation tools. The blocking is deep and technically sophisticated. You need a VPN with active obfuscation.

**Russia**: Social media, news sites (BBC, Deutsche Welle, Voice of America), many Western services, and VPN provider websites themselves. If you use Google Workspace for work, some Google services are accessible but others are restricted.

**UAE**: VoIP is the main issue. WhatsApp calls, FaceTime, Zoom video, Skype all blocked. Standard web browsing, social media, and most apps work fine. Political and religious content is blocked but unlikely to affect most travelers.

**Turkey**: Intermittent social media blocks during political events. Most content accessible most of the time, but be prepared for outages.

**Iran**: Very heavy censorship. Instagram, Twitter, WhatsApp, Telegram have all faced blocking. State monitoring is real. If you're a journalist or activist, get professional-grade tools, not just a commercial VPN.

**Belarus, Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan**: Intermittent blocking, often mirroring Russian patterns. Less technically sophisticated than China or Russia but unpredictable.

## Step 2: Pick Two VPNs From Two Different Providers

Every experienced traveler uses two VPNs, not two subscriptions to the same company. Here's why:

When the Great Firewall or Roskomnadzor's TSPU blocks a VPN, they often block an entire provider's server ranges and infrastructure at once. If your primary VPN goes down, your backup from the same company uses the same blocked infrastructure. Two different providers mean two independent chances at connectivity.

**Primary recommendation**: NordVPN. Largest server network, strong obfuscation options, automatic protocol switching, apps on every platform. Works in UAE, Turkey, and Russia. Works in China with manual configuration of obfuscated servers.

**Secondary recommendation**: Proton VPN. Swiss jurisdiction, audited no-logs policy, Stealth protocol for high-censorship environments, free tier available. Their server network is smaller than Nord but their obfuscation approach is technically distinct.

Alternatives: ExpressVPN (strong automatic obfuscation, especially for China), Mullvad (best for Russia specifically, WireGuard-over-QUIC obfuscation), Astrill (best for China and Russia, most expensive).

## Step 3: Configure Obfuscation Before You Leave

Default VPN settings use standard protocols that are easily blocked in censored countries. Change these settings before you travel:

**NordVPN**: Go to Settings > Advanced > Obfuscated Servers. Toggle this on. The app will now only connect through obfuscated servers. Pre-select and favorite several server locations in nearby regions (for China: Hong Kong, Japan, Singapore; for Russia: Finland, Latvia, Netherlands).

**Proton VPN**: Go to Settings and set Protocol to Stealth. This wraps WireGuard in TLS 1.3 obfuscation. Test it before you leave by enabling it while on your home network and confirming everything still works.

**ExpressVPN**: The Lightway protocol includes automatic obfuscation. Select Lightway as the protocol. In China specifically, the app has a dedicated "China mode" in some versions that enables additional obfuscation layers.

**Kill switch**: Enable the kill switch on whichever VPN you're using. This disconnects your internet entirely if the VPN drops, rather than falling back to your unprotected connection. In censored countries, falling back to the unprotected connection can expose your traffic or get you cut off from apps that assume VPN protection.

## Step 4: Pre-Download Alternative Apps

For China specifically, install alternatives to your blocked apps before you leave:

- **Maps**: Google Maps doesn't work in China. Download Apple Maps (works) and the Amap or Baidu Maps app (works without VPN, in Chinese).
- **Translation**: Download Google Translate and cache offline language packs (Chinese, Russian, Arabic) while you have normal internet access.
- **Communication**: WeChat is mandatory for China. Create an account before you arrive. Your contacts in China will not be reachable through WhatsApp or iMessage. WeChat requires a phone number to register.
- **Payments**: Cash is important in China. Alipay and WeChat Pay are ubiquitous but require a Chinese bank account or Alipay international onboarding (limited availability).
- **Email**: If you use Gmail, VPN access to it will work. Consider downloading emails for offline access in case of connectivity gaps.

For Russia:
- Telegram is the dominant messaging platform and generally accessible
- VK (VKontakte) is the dominant social platform
- Yandex Maps works without VPN

## Step 5: Test Everything on Your Home Network

This step gets skipped constantly and causes problems. Before you leave:

1. Enable your VPN in obfuscation mode
2. Connect to a server in the destination region or a nearby country
3. Test: can you open your work email? Does Slack connect? Does your company's remote access tool work?
4. Test your backup VPN with the same steps
5. Test the kill switch: enable it, then disconnect the VPN manually, and confirm your internet drops rather than staying connected unprotected

Some corporate VPN and zero-trust network access tools don't work well inside a consumer VPN tunnel. Better to find this out at home than when you're trying to join a call in a Beijing hotel room.

## Step 6: Know the Local Laws

A quick summary of where VPNs are legally problematic:

**China**: Technically illegal without government approval for residents, but tourists are not prosecuted. Download and use without worry.

**UAE**: Legal to use. Illegal to use for accessing prohibited content. Fines are large on paper. Enforcement against tourists for normal use is essentially zero.

**Russia**: Legal for individuals. Illegal for providers to advertise bypass services. You're fine.

**Iran**: Legal grey area. VPN use by individuals is widespread and not actively prosecuted.

**Turkey**: Legal. Not a legal risk.

**North Korea**: Don't go without a government guide. Irrelevant VPN discussion.

Countries where VPNs are genuinely illegal: Belarus (somewhat), Turkmenistan, Iraq (inconsistent). For these, research current conditions before traveling.

## Step 7: Download an eSIM as a Backup

Hotel WiFi and local SIM cards in censored countries both have the same ISP-level restrictions applied to them. But an eSIM from a global provider like Airalo or Holafly routes your connection through international data networks that are less aggressively filtered.

This is not a VPN replacement but a useful secondary option. In many cases a global eSIM data plan bypasses local DNS and IP blocks that apply only to domestic ISPs. For WhatsApp calling in the UAE, some travelers find an international eSIM works without a VPN at all, because the traffic routes through international infrastructure before hitting UAE telecoms.

## Our Top Picks for Travel

**NordVPN** covers the widest range of restrictive countries with reliable performance. Buy a plan before you travel, install both the mobile and desktop apps, enable obfuscated servers, and pre-save servers in target regions.

**Proton VPN** as a backup or standalone for privacy-conscious travelers. The free tier works for backup use. The Stealth protocol handles China, Russia, and UAE DPI reliably.
